senses.gloss: inclining; inclining from one religion to another; belonging to the right religion, of true belief
inclining; inclining from one religion to another; someone connected to a wrong religion, impious, deviatory

etymology.text: Of the root ح ن ف (ḥ n f). Probably an Aramaic loan, borrowing first the plural of Classical Syriac ܚܢܦܐ (ḥanpā, “pagan, impious”) and back-forming the Arabic singular, or from a differently vocalized Nabataean Aramaic relative of it, the details are disputed, like the semantics were a source of disagreement from the beginning.

body.text: (Akh, S, TA;) thus applied in like manner as
is applied to a crow: (S:) [and particularly]
inclining, from one religion, to another:
inclining, from any false religion, to the true religion:
inclining in a perfect manner to El-Islám, and continuing firm therein:
(As, K, TA:) so say I'Ab and El-Hasan and Es-Suddee; and Az says the like on the authority of Ed-Dahhák: (TA:) or one
who is of the religion of Abraham,
in respect of making the Sacred House
his kibleh, and of the rite of circumcision:

(S, Mgh, Msb;) because he inclines to the right religion: (Msb:) but in this last sense, it is a conventional term of the professors: (Mgh:) [or,] accord. to AO, the worshipper of idols, in the Time of Ignorance, called himself thus; and when El-Islám came, they thus called the Muslim: accord. to Akh, it was applied in the Time of Ignorance to him
who was circumcised, and who performed the pilgrimage to the
because the Arabs in the Time of Ignorance held nothing of the religion of Abraham except circumcision and that pilgrimage: accord. to Ez-Zejjájee, it was applied in the Time of Ignorance to him
who made the pilgrimage to the
House and performed the ablution on account of
and when El-Islám came, it was applied to the Muslim, because of his turning from the belief in a plurality of gods: (TA:) also one
who devotes himself to religious exercises;
(Msb:) its predominant application is to Abraham: (Mgh:) pl.
of the time of El-Islám; not old.
